title: Reader Type Selector Guide
summary: Understand how reader choice affects credential compatibility, security level, user experience, and future migration.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool helps compare reader choices based on credential type, environment, security expectations, and upgrade path.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it before selecting readers for new installs, expansions, migrations, or sites moving away from legacy proximity credentials.
bullets: Early design planning.
Expansion review.
Existing-system cleanup or validation.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions explain what each input represents in practical access-control terms.

items.label: Credential Type
items.description: The credential technology users will present to the reader.
items.whyItMatters: Credential type determines reader compatibility and security level.

items.label: Security Level
items.description: The level of resistance needed against cloning or misuse.
items.whyItMatters: Higher-security areas may justify encrypted smart credentials.
items.commonMistake: Using legacy proximity credentials everywhere by default.

items.label: Environment
items.description: The physical condition where the reader will be installed.
items.whyItMatters: Outdoor or harsh environments may require different reader ratings.
items.commonMistake: Using indoor readers outdoors without checking ratings.

items.label: User Experience
items.description: How users interact with the reader.
items.whyItMatters: Reader type affects speed, convenience, and support calls.
items.commonMistake: Choosing security features that users will bypass or misuse.

items.label: Compatibility
items.description: Whether the reader works with the existing panel and credentials.
items.whyItMatters: Compatibility affects wiring, encryption, and migration options.
items.commonMistake: Assuming a new reader works with old cards and old panels.

items.label: Migration Path
items.description: How the site may move from old credentials to newer ones.
items.whyItMatters: Migration planning prevents forced rip-and-replace later.
items.commonMistake: Buying single-tech readers that block future upgrades.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the reader type is likely aligned with the application. Watch means compatibility or future migration should be reviewed. Risk means the reader selection may create security, usability, or upgrade problems.
bullets: Healthy: the design appears manageable based on the entered assumptions.
Watch: the design may still work, but the assumptions deserve review.
Risk: simplify the design, add capacity, or validate the design more carefully before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most access-control design problems come from underestimating how hardware, users, schedules, exceptions, and maintenance effort interact over time.
bullets: Designing only for the initial install instead of the system lifecycle.
Ignoring exceptions, expansion, or administrative workload.
Assuming manufacturer capacity limits are the same as practical design limits.
Failing to document assumptions before the system is built.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, platform-specific limits, code review, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Manufacturer limits still matter.
Local code and life-safety requirements still apply.
Site conditions may change the final design.
A simpler design is usually easier to support, audit, and explain.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, code compliance review, manufacturer validation, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual product and platform being installed.
Confirm owner requirements and operating procedures.
Document assumptions before implementation.
